EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light, fluffy one to two inches of snow fell on top of the snowcover from the February 16 winter storm. The new snow produced a fresh coating on top of already cleared roads, and additional snow on top of unplowed streets and back roads. Gusty winds caused some blowing of the snow. A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. The fast-moving disturbance had little moisture to work with, but it was strong enough to squeeze out minor snow accumulations.

The residents of eastern Kentucky experienced one of the most significant winter storms in recent memory on February 16. The snow first began falling in our far western counties between 3:00 and 3:30 am on February 16, 2015. The snow slowly spread eastward throughout the morning and eventually ended during the early overnight hours of February 17th. The final storm totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches along portions of the Tennessee and southwestern Virginia borders, to as much as 12 to 15 inches along a central portion of the area from Estill county all the way over to Pike county. A large portion of the area received between 4 and 11 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer weather observer measured 1.6 inches of snow in Wellington at 7:00 am to mark the start of this major storm. Moderate to at times heavy snow fell throughout the day and into the evening. The storm finally began winding down around 10 pm. The highest final storm snow fall totals were measured in Frenchburg (11 inches) and Wellington (11.7 inches). Around 10 inches of snow had fallen across the county on average by the time the storm finally came to an end.

The snow first began falling in our far western counties between 3:00 and 3:30 am on February 16, 2015. The snow slowly spread eastward throughout the morning and eventually ended during the early overnight hours of February 17th. The final storm totals ranged from 2 to 4 inches along portions of the Tennessee and southwestern Virginia borders, to as much as 12 to 15 inches along a central portion of the area from Estill county all the way over to Pike county. A large portion of the area received between 4 and 11 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called in the first snow fall report of the day out Floyd county, with a measurement of 3.5 inches at 825 am. By 2 pm on this day, around 7 inches of snow had already accumulated in areas such as Allen, Prestonsburg, and Harold. By the time the storm finally ran out of steam, 11 to 14 inches of snow had fallen around the county. The highest snow totals were as follows: 14 inches 2 miles north of Prestonsburg, 11 inches 5 miles west of Prestonsburg, 14 inches at Grethel, 13 inches at Harold, and 12 inches in Melvin atop Abner Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist air mass lifted north from the Gulf of Mexico and interacted with a front, which oscillated across the region for several days. Periods of heavy rain fell across the region is response to this setup, producing between one and three inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that Gunpowder Creek was out of its' banks in Florence near Gunpowder Road and U.S. 42.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter inch ice accumulations occurred around the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harlan county was particularly hard hit by this storm. Between 944 am and 3 pm on the 21st, 4 to 12 inches snow had fallen across the county. The highest snowfall total of 12 inches occurred a few miles south of Benham. The city of Harlan received 8 inches of snow. The other major issue caused by this storm was roof collapses that resulted from heavy wet snow piling up on buildings and homes. Locations that experienced this type of damage were the Gulf/Commissary gas station in Harlan where the station canopy collapsed, and the Guthrie Memorial Airport where a partial roof and structure collapse occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of wet snow coupled with several inches of rain resulted in Stoner Creek at Paris to rise above flood stage. The river crested at 21.0 feet early on March 5th then fell below flood stage late on March 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One lane of KY 550 at mile marker 9 was impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by 1 to 2 inches of rain caused the Rough River at Dundee to rise above flood stage. The river crested at 26.97 feet during the afternoon of March 5th and fell below flood stage the next afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter of an inch of ice due to freezing rain was reported in Hagerhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed along an old thunderstorm outflow boundary over southern Illinois. The storms expanded in coverage as they moved southeast into western Kentucky. A few storms produced gusty winds and hail. The storms congealed into a mass of heavy rain over the Owensboro area, resulting in an urban flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few reports of quarter-size hail on the north side of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One hundred yards of six inch deep water was flowing over Spencer Rd on Rt 713. A lot of drive ways in the area were washed out.

In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adams Ridge on Brush Branch had to be closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Aberdeen, 15.6 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Hwy 223 was closed between mile markers 7 and 8 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were damaged, causing a utility line to fall onto North Milo Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another subtle weather disturbance pushed through the Ohio Valley on the afternoon and evening of April 8th. This disturbance interacted with a very moist and unstable airmass, characterized by temperatures in the upper 70s and 80s. Supercell thunderstorms developed across parts of the area, dropping hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed to the NWS that penny size hail fell in White City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms streaked through northeast Kentucky ahead of a board mid level trough in the upper Midwest.  This was also hundreds of miles ahead of the surface cold front. The first line moved through near midday, while another moved through during the early evening.  Their movement was toward the northeast at 40 to 45 mph.  Gusty winds and downpours were common. The rain was beneficial.  However, a few storms pulsed briefly to stronger levels during the midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down.

Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 11 inches of snow was measured near Roseville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of storm total snow fall occurred across portions of Wayne county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 8 to 13 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms intensified across western Kentucky during the afternoon. The storms then propagated slowly southeastward through a moderately unstable environment, with mixed-layer capes around 2000 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear values of 30 to 40 knots were favorable for organized convection. Damaging wind gusts accompanied the strongest storms, and some isolated large hail was reported. The storms formed along and ahead of a weakening convective outflow boundary as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Some places were in the path of two or three clusters of storms. The resulting heavy rainfall totals over moist ground resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of the hail was posted on a social media website. Hail slightly larger than golf balls occurred in extreme southwest McCracken County, from the Ballard County line near Lovelaceville to two miles northeast of Lovelaceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter inch ice accumulations occurred around the county. The roof of a store collapsed in Beattyville due to the weight of heavy wet snow that had accumulated on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms streaked through northeast Kentucky ahead of a board mid level trough in the upper Midwest.  This was also hundreds of miles ahead of the surface cold front. The first line moved through near midday, while another moved through during the early evening.  Their movement was toward the northeast at 40 to 45 mph.  Gusty winds and downpours were common. The rain was beneficial.  However, a few storms pulsed briefly to stronger levels during the midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell down near the intersection of Ced Gap Road and KY 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed during the afternoon in southern Ohio, near an east to west frontal zone.  Those storms moved east into western West Virginia.  During the evening, new storms formed further south, affecting northeast Kentucky.

In the late afternoon, a low level boundary existed along the Ohio River from near Cincinnati to near Ashland, then east into central West Virginia. Rotating thunderstorms formed along this boundary, the strongest of which was across the Ohio River in southern Ohio. Downpours occurred across a narrow section of Greenup County causing minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 827 was flooded and closed along Coal Branch.  No dwellings were known to flood. Rain estimates were around 2 inches in 1 to 2 hours.

Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 367, Newman Rd, and Hwy 1200 were affected by high water but not closed. These are low spots that are flood prone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another subtle weather disturbance pushed through the Ohio Valley on the afternoon and evening of April 8th. This disturbance interacted with a very moist and unstable airmass, characterized by temperatures in the upper 70s and 80s. Supercell thunderstorms developed across parts of the area, dropping hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail near Pulliam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on various surfaces and objects throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 1261 was flooded and impassable at the intersection of Spring Branch Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of eastern Kentucky experienced more flashing flooding on April 7, 2015. The hardest areas were Estill and Knox counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on Hwy 89 at the city limits with water also over the road on Hwy 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another subtle weather disturbance pushed through the Ohio Valley on the afternoon and evening of April 8th. This disturbance interacted with a very moist and unstable airmass, characterized by temperatures in the upper 70s and 80s. Supercell thunderstorms developed across parts of the area, dropping hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime size hail fell at Bowman Field in Louisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms slowly increased during the midday hours in advance of a mesoscale convective vortex approaching from southeast Missouri. Partial sunshine resulted in moderate instability, with mixed-layer cape values near 1000 j/kg. Winds aloft were unfavorable for organized storm structures, with deep-layer shear only near 20 knots. The combination of moderate instability and relatively high precipitable water values resulted in an isolated wet microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few metal panels were blown off a small garage. Power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms intensified across western Kentucky during the afternoon. The storms then propagated slowly southeastward through a moderately unstable environment, with mixed-layer capes around 2000 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear values of 30 to 40 knots were favorable for organized convection. Damaging wind gusts accompanied the strongest storms, and some isolated large hail was reported. The storms formed along and ahead of a weakening convective outflow boundary as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Some places were in the path of two or three clusters of storms. The resulting heavy rainfall totals over moist ground resulted in isolated flash flooding.

Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to three tenths of an inch of ice accumulated on various surfaces and objects around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three feet of water was covering Hwy 613 near Bowen.

Supercell thunderstorms developed across parts of the area, dropping hail up to 2 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky 1426 was flooded at Spears Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated damaging wind gusts occurred with bands of northeastward moving thunderstorms. Weak to moderate instability and deep, unidirectional southwesterly flow aloft helped to maintain the storms. A 500 mb shortwave trough over the Ozarks moved northeast to the vicinity of the Ohio and Mississippi River confluence by mid-afternoon. A weak surface low pressure center moved northeast from southeast Missouri into south central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smaller trees were split due to high wind. A large tree was down on a home, causing some minor damage to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 7 to 10 inches of snow fell countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the heavy snow, heavy rain and subsequent snow melt of March 4-5, additional rainfall March 7 - 11 continued minor river flooding across central Kentucky. This resulted in the Ohio River to rise above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on top of already saturated ground pushed the Stoner Creek at Paris above flood stage. The river crested at 19.5 feet during the evening of March 10th, then fell below flood stage in the late afternoon on March 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of complexes of thunderstorms affected western Kentucky during the early morning hours. This complex was associated with the nose of a strong southwest low-level jet. A few damaging wind gusts were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three mobile homes sustained roof or siding damage. Several trees or tree limbs were down. A small storage shed was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across the area brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the lower Ohio Valley April 2nd and 3rd. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours April 2nd, resulting in large hail and damaging winds. Later that evening and into the early morning hours of April 3rd, training thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Kentucky, greatly impacting the I-64 corridor including the Louisville and Lexington metros. Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in the 2 day period and April 3rd ended up being Louisville's 4th wettest day on record. The event garnered national media attention as over 160 water rescues occurred in Louisville alone and several roads were washed out across northern Kentucky. Additional showers and thunderstorms then developed during the afternoon of April 3rd with isolated severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought the Kentucky River at Lockport into flood, cresting at 43.04 feet on the morning of April 5th.

The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jackson county experienced a plethora of winter weather types during this event. The Dept of Highways reported that an estimated quarter of an inch of ice had accumulated on various objects and surfaces during the storm. Three to five inches of snow and sleet also were reported at various locations through out the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by 1 to 2 inches of rain resulted in the Green River at Brownsville to rise above flood stage. The river crested at 23.48 feet late on March 6th and then fell below flood stage late on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the heavy snow, heavy rain and subsequent snow melt of March 4-5, additional rainfall March 7 - 11 continued minor river flooding across central Kentucky. This resulted in the Ohio River to rise above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on top of already saturated ground pushed the Licking River at Blue Licks Spring above flood stage, cresting at 27.91 feet during the early morning hours of March 11th. The river fell below flood stage during the afternoon of March 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of complexes of thunderstorms affected western Kentucky during the early morning hours. The first complex was associated with a convective outflow boundary that moved southeast from southern Illinois. Hailstones up to nickel-size and local street flooding were observed with these storms. Another complex of storms moved east from southeast Missouri into western Kentucky later in the morning. This complex was associated with the nose of a strong southwest low-level jet. A few damaging wind gusts were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fewer than a dozen trees or tree limbs were blown down, including three large trees. There was damage to the roof and gutters of two homes. A number of homes lost shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 at the 1086 junction was down to one lane due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms intensified across western Kentucky during the afternoon. The storms then propagated slowly southeastward through a moderately unstable environment, with mixed-layer capes around 2000 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear values of 30 to 40 knots were favorable for organized convection. Damaging wind gusts accompanied the strongest storms, and some isolated large hail was reported. The storms formed along and ahead of a weakening convective outflow boundary as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Some places were in the path of two or three clusters of storms. The resulting heavy rainfall totals over moist ground resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across the area brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the lower Ohio Valley April 2nd and 3rd. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours April 2nd, resulting in large hail and damaging winds. Later that evening and into the early morning hours of April 3rd, training thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Kentucky, greatly impacting the I-64 corridor including the Louisville and Lexington metros. Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in the 2 day period and April 3rd ended up being Louisville's 4th wettest day on record. The event garnered national media attention as over 160 water rescues occurred in Louisville alone and several roads were washed out across northern Kentucky. Additional showers and thunderstorms then developed during the afternoon of April 3rd with isolated severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought the Green River at Rochester into flood. The river was estimated to have crested at 17.6 feet early April 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system interacted with a warm and unstable airmass in the late afternoon and evening of April 7th to produce severe weather over portions of central Kentucky.  Wind damage and large hail were common with the strongest storms and 2 tornadoes occurred in Madison County. The heavy rain that fell, already on top of saturated ground, brought a few rivers into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down near a ravine just northwest of Jessica Circle where it snapped a few trees. It then moved southeast, destroying two outbuildings. One of the outbuildings was fully packed, weighing several thousand pounds, and another larger outbuilding was pushed off its foundation. The tornado also did some damage to the roof and shingles of a residence at 326 Jessica Circle. The tornado then continued on an intermittent path to the southeast where it did some additional roof damage to another home and snapped several other trees. The tornado lifted just west of Cherokee Lane.

Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 15 had a foot of standing water over it and Hwy 613 had 5 feet of water over it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated damaging wind gusts occurred with bands of northeastward moving thunderstorms. Weak to moderate instability and deep, unidirectional southwesterly flow aloft helped to maintain the storms. A 500 mb shortwave trough over the Ozarks moved northeast to the vicinity of the Ohio and Mississippi River confluence by mid-afternoon. A weak surface low pressure center moved northeast from southeast Missouri into south central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Eddyville, large tree branches were down, along with some power lines. A trampoline was blown about 200 yards. At the intersection of U.S. Highways 641 and 62, debris was blown into power lines. Trees were down on U.S. Highway 641 just north of Eddyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the heavy snow, heavy rain and subsequent snow melt of March 4-5, additional rainfall March 7 - 11 continued minor river flooding across central Kentucky. This resulted in the Ohio River to rise above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain falling on top of already saturated ground pushed the Rolling Fork River at Boston above flood stage. The river crested at 39.12 feet during the morning of March 12th and then fell below flood stage in the morning hours of March 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 was closed due to flooding from Burning Fork Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms intensified across western Kentucky during the afternoon. The storms then propagated slowly southeastward through a moderately unstable environment, with mixed-layer capes around 2000 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear values of 30 to 40 knots were favorable for organized convection. Damaging wind gusts accompanied the strongest storms, and some isolated large hail was reported. The storms formed along and ahead of a weakening convective outflow boundary as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Some places were in the path of two or three clusters of storms. The resulting heavy rainfall totals over moist ground resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms with intense lightning developed in a broad southerly flow of warm and humid air. Lightning struck a house near Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning in a neighborhood just southeast of the Paducah city limits. The resulting house fire caused extensive damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill produced isolated tornadoes and locally strong winds. The convective character of the bands increased during the afternoon due to short intervals of sunshine that destabilized the atmosphere. The surface low pressure center that constituted the remnants of Bill was located over the extreme southeast corner of Missouri during the afternoon. Rain bands rotated northward across western Kentucky ahead of the low pressure center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system interacted with a warm and unstable airmass in the late afternoon and evening of April 7th to produce severe weather over portions of central Kentucky.  Wind damage and large hail were common with the strongest storms and 2 tornadoes occurred in Madison County. The heavy rain that fell, already on top of saturated ground, brought a few rivers into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highway officials reported that a high water rescue took place on O'nan Bend Rd as a car became submerged in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system interacted with a warm and unstable airmass in the late afternoon and evening of April 7th to produce severe weather over portions of central Kentucky.  Wind damage and large hail were common with the strongest storms and 2 tornadoes occurred in Madison County. The heavy rain that fell, already on top of saturated ground, brought a few rivers into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down on a farm at 1235 Crooksville Road where it snapped and uprooted several trees, spreading them in several directions. It then continued southeast across Gumbottom Road, where a large oak tree was uprooted. Sporadic tree damage continued southeast towards Walter Lakes Road. Just before the tornado lifted, it did minor shingle and porch damage to a residence along Walter Lakes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across the area brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the lower Ohio Valley April 2nd and 3rd. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours April 2nd, resulting in large hail and damaging winds. Later that evening and into the early morning hours of April 3rd, training thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Kentucky, greatly impacting the I-64 corridor including the Louisville and Lexington metros. Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in the 2 day period and April 3rd ended up being Louisville's 4th wettest day on record. The event garnered national media attention as over 160 water rescues occurred in Louisville alone and several roads were washed out across northern Kentucky. Additional showers and thunderstorms then developed during the afternoon of April 3rd with isolated severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought the Green River at Woodbury into flood, cresting at 34.0 feet on the evening of April 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill produced isolated tornadoes and locally strong winds. The convective character of the bands increased during the afternoon due to short intervals of sunshine that destabilized the atmosphere. The surface low pressure center that constituted the remnants of Bill was located over the extreme southeast corner of Missouri during the afternoon. Rain bands rotated northward across western Kentucky ahead of the low pressure center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and tree limbs were blown down. Swaths of corn and wheat were blown down in farm fields. The tornado began along Kentucky Highway 80 near Farmington, then moved northeast across Highway 564. Peak winds were estimated by a damage survey team near 75 mph. The average path width was about 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple city and county roads were being impacted by flash flooding and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across the area brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the lower Ohio Valley April 2nd and 3rd. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours April 2nd, resulting in large hail and damaging winds. Later that evening and into the early morning hours of April 3rd, training thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Kentucky, greatly impacting the I-64 corridor including the Louisville and Lexington metros. Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in the 2 day period and April 3rd ended up being Louisville's 4th wettest day on record. The event garnered national media attention as over 160 water rescues occurred in Louisville alone and several roads were washed out across northern Kentucky. Additional showers and thunderstorms then developed during the afternoon of April 3rd with isolated severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system interacted with a warm and unstable airmass in the late afternoon and evening of April 7th to produce severe weather over portions of central Kentucky.  Wind damage and large hail were common with the strongest storms and 2 tornadoes occurred in Madison County. The heavy rain that fell, already on top of saturated ground, brought a few rivers into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought Stoner Creek at Paris above flood stage on April 7th. The river crested at 19.4 feet early on April 8th before dropping below flood stage later that same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dark Hollow Rd was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by 1 to 2 inches of rain resulted in the Kentucky River at Lockport to rise above flood stage. The river crested at 37.69 feet during the early morning hours of March 9th. The river remained above flood stage until the morning hours of March 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity over western Kentucky. A moist and unstable atmosphere, coupled with favorable winds aloft, resulted in isolated large hail and damaging winds. The storms developed along and ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local fire department reported the ground was covered with quarter-size to half dollar-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front sparked a round of strong to severe thunderstorms across central Kentucky. Storms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours and produced sporadic severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down by the public, along Green Ridge Spa Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destructive thunderstorm winds estimated up to 95 mph snapped or uprooted dozens of large trees. A mobile home had major damage to walls on one end. At least a half dozen homes had mainly minor shingle and siding damage. One barn was leveled, with at least ten others receiving some loss of roof and siding. Most of the damage occurred along and near Highways 106, 171, and a couple of rural county roads. The path length was six miles, and the path width was about two miles.

At least a dozen trees were uprooted or snapped. Dozens of tree limbs were blown down. Areas of wheat and corn crops were blown over. At least one shed was damaged by a falling tree. The path width of the microburst was about a third of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed along an old thunderstorm outflow boundary over southern Illinois. The storms expanded in coverage as they moved southeast into western Kentucky. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many photos of multiple flooded roads throughout Owensboro were posted on social media. The automated rain gage at the Owensboro airport measured 1.96 inches in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky 541 near the Mountain Gospel radio station was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought flooding rains to central Kentucky, followed quickly by exceptionally heavy snow. This amount of rain, followed by such heavy snow, is practically unprecedented.   The upper level pattern featured a positively tilted upper trough across the desert southwest on the 3nd of March. A tight baroclinic zone stretched northeastward through southern Indiana. Strong southwesterly flow at lower levels brought rich moisture along this nearly stationary boundary. Initially, during the evening hours on the 3nd, rain developed along this boundary and gradually overspread all of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Steady rain continued through the late afternoon on the 4th. Two to almost 3 inches of rain fell across north central and central Kentucky before precipitation changed into snow during the late afternoon hours on the 4th. Minor areal flooding developed with several roads and low water crossings closed. ||Rain changed into heavy snow near the Ohio River around 5pm, with precipitation changeover slowly moving farther south during the evening, Rain finally changed over to snow near the Tennessee Border during the early morning hours.  Intense frontogenesis and lift associated with the right rear quadrant of a powerful jet led to the development of several intense snow bands where snow fell at a rate of 2 inches per hour. One band developed from near Breckenridge County and stretched through Bullitt County and across the northern Bluegrass. Under this nearly stationary band, snow totals ranged from 15 to locally over 20 inches. One reliable snow report from near Radcliff, Kentucky measured 25 inches, which is one inch short of the all time Kentucky storm total snowfall record. Snow diminished from west to east during the mid-morning hours on the 5th. Snow totals across south central Kentucky, adjacent to Tennessee, ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by 1 to 2 inches of rain resulted in the Green River at Rochester to rise above flood stage. The river was estimated to have crested at 22.2 feet during the evening hours of March 16th. The river dropped below flood stage early on March 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major winter storm wreaked havoc across eastern Kentucky February 20th and 21st. This storm caused significant ice and snow accumulations across the area, along with flooding in several counties around the area. The bulk of the snow was confined to the counties along the Virginia border, where more than a foot of heavy wet snow fell in places. The heavy snow caused extensive damage as it caused the roofs of a number of homes and other structures to collapse. The ice lead to a number of car accidents and made driving quite treacherous at times. A total of 10 eastern Kentucky counties experienced flooding due to heavy rain and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was the issue in Knox county during this event. A quarter of an inch of ice had accumulated in Barbourville by 8 am on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms intensified across western Kentucky during the afternoon. The storms then propagated slowly southeastward through a moderately unstable environment, with mixed-layer capes around 2000 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear values of 30 to 40 knots were favorable for organized convection. Damaging wind gusts accompanied the strongest storms, and some isolated large hail was reported. The storms formed along and ahead of a weakening convective outflow boundary as it moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Some places were in the path of two or three clusters of storms. The resulting heavy rainfall totals over moist ground resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 45 was flooded. Water was reported over some other roads, as well.

Clusters of showers and thunderstorms began affecting central and eastern Kentucky during the pre-dawn hours of Friday April 3rd.  The initial complex of thunderstorms targeted areas from Louisville across the Bluegrass Region in parts of east-central Kentucky, including the Natural Bridge State Park area.  While these storms were not severe, they did produce gusty winds which downed a dead tree onto a camping site in the Middle Fork Campground killing a female camper and severely injuring another person. ||Torrential rainfall occurred with the storms, with many locations in a stripe from Estill, Powell, Lee, Wolfe and Breathitt Counties seeing from three to four inches of rain during the morning storms.  This caused widespread flash flooding and a Lee County woman was killed as she tried to drive through flood waters. ||After a brief break, additional showers and thunderstorms targeted locations from Lexington eastward along I-64 into the Gateway Region Friday evening.  This again caused widespread flash flooding in a stripe from Montgomery, Bath, Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the road on Hwy 460 near the Menifee/Montgomery county line. the road was not closed at the time of the report. There was also flooding reported in low spots on Sawmill Rd and Davis Rd in Jeffersonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across the area brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to the lower Ohio Valley April 2nd and 3rd. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours April 2nd, resulting in large hail and damaging winds. Later that evening and into the early morning hours of April 3rd, training thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Kentucky, greatly impacting the I-64 corridor including the Louisville and Lexington metros. Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in the 2 day period and April 3rd ended up being Louisville's 4th wettest day on record. The event garnered national media attention as over 160 water rescues occurred in Louisville alone and several roads were washed out across northern Kentucky. Additional showers and thunderstorms then developed during the afternoon of April 3rd with isolated severe wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the heavy snow, heavy rain and subsequent snow melt of March 4-5, additional rainfall March 7 - 11 continued minor river flooding across central Kentucky. This resulted in the Ohio River to rise above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in the Ohio River at McAlpine Lower to go into a long-duration flood. The river crested at 61.29 feet on March 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major late winter storm affected the state of Kentucky from late in the day on Tuesday March 3rd through the morning of Thursday March 5th, 2015.  ||The storm began as rain overspread east Kentucky from late Tuesday March 3rd into the day of Wednesday March 4th, falling heavily at times.  The rain, coupled with a melting high elevation snow pack containing considerable water content, caused extensive runoff and produced widespread flooding of small creeks and streams and poor draining areas during the day on Wednesday.  Some of the hardest hit areas on Wednesday were in Harlan and Bell Counties, where runoff quickly pushed the creeks and streams there over their banks.  ||The flooding then progressed into our rivers resulting in some of the worst river flooding in over a decade.  In fact, on the Big Sandy, several locations experienced the highest water since 1984.  The most noteworthy crest occurred on the Cumberland River at Pineville, which based on preliminary information recorded the 3rd highest crest on record.  ||As area rivers where rising, the precipitation changed to sleet and then snow Wednesday night.  An especially heavy band of snow set up from western Kentucky northeastward through central Kentucky into northeast Kentucky.  By midday Thursday, over a foot of snow had fallen in this band mainly along and north of I-64 in east Kentucky.  The snowfall amounts dropped significantly to the south with generally 2 to 4 along with up to a half inch of sleet having fallen near the Tennessee border.  The snow diminished during the midday hours on Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel Fork Lane was partially flooded by Rockhouse Creek.
